requirements.summary: The character data in a UTF-8 Encoded String MUST be well-formed UTF-8 as defined by the Unicode specification and restated in RFC 3629 . In particular, the character data MUST NOT include encodings of code points between U+D800 and U+DFFF .
requirements.mapping_reason: Shared validation in validate_mqtt_string checks well-formed UTF-8 without allocating. read_mqtt_string uses that validator, and Publish.topic plus LastWill.topic validate their Bytes-backed topic fields after read_mqtt_bytes and before write_mqtt_bytes while preserving zero-copy storage.

evidence.reason: validate_mqtt_string uses std::str::from_utf8() to reject ill-formed UTF-8 including surrogates (U+D800-U+DFFF). read_mqtt_string delegates to it, and the v5 write_mqtt_string wrapper validates before serializing.
Publish.topic remains Bytes, but Publish::read validates the topic bytes immediately after read_mqtt_bytes and Publish::write validates them before write_mqtt_bytes.
LastWill.topic remains Bytes, but LastWill::read validates the will topic bytes immediately after read_mqtt_bytes and LastWill::write validates them before write_mqtt_bytes.
Runtime receiver handling maps TopicNotUtf8 decode errors to a Malformed Packet DISCONNECT reason.

test_coverage.notes: Core string decoding tests cover malformed UTF-8 and surrogate encodings. v5 packet tests cover Bytes-backed Publish.topic and LastWill.topic validation. The framed runtime test verifies TopicNotUtf8 is handled as a Malformed Packet DISCONNECT.

requirements.summary: A UTF-8 Encoded String MUST NOT include an encoding of the null character U+0000. . If a receiver (Server or Client) receives an MQTT Control Packet containing U+0000 it is a Malformed Packet. Refer to section 4.13 for information about handling errors.
requirements.mapping_reason: validate_mqtt_string rejects U+0000 after well-formed UTF-8 validation. read_mqtt_string delegates to it, the v5 write_mqtt_string wrapper validates all outgoing UTF-8 string fields, and Bytes-backed Publish.topic and LastWill.topic validate on both read and write.

evidence.reason: validate_mqtt_string rejects literal U+0000 bytes with Error::MalformedPacket. read_mqtt_string delegates to it, and the v5 write_mqtt_string wrapper validates before serializing.
Publish.topic is validated after read_mqtt_bytes and before write_mqtt_bytes, so Bytes-backed topic storage cannot bypass the U+0000 check.
client_id and other String-backed fields use read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string validation. LastWill.topic is validated on both read and write.

test_coverage.notes: Tests cover literal U+0000 rejection in the shared string reader plus Bytes-backed Publish.topic and LastWill.topic read/write paths.

requirements.summary: A UTF-8 encoded sequence 0xEF 0xBB 0xBF is always interpreted as U+FEFF ("ZERO WIDTH NO-BREAK SPACE") wherever it appears in a string and MUST NOT be skipped over or stripped off by a packet receiver .
requirements.mapping_reason: read_mqtt_string uses std::str::from_utf8() followed by to_owned(), which preserves the BOM (0xEF 0xBB 0xBF = U+FEFF) as a regular character. There is no stripping or skipping logic anywhere in the decode path. write_mqtt_string writes the string bytes as-is via write_mqtt_bytes, preserving the BOM. The implementation naturally satisfies this requirement by never manipulating decoded string content.

evidence.reason: read_mqtt_string calls from_utf8() then to_owned() with no filtering or stripping. The BOM sequence 0xEF 0xBB 0xBF decodes to U+FEFF and is preserved. write_mqtt_string delegates to write_mqtt_bytes which writes raw bytes without modification.
v5 module re-exports core primitives directly with no wrapping or post-processing that could strip the BOM.

test_coverage.notes: A targeted test verifies that read_mqtt_string preserves a leading U+FEFF character.

requirements.summary: The Variable Byte Integer is encoded using an encoding scheme which uses a single byte for values up to 127. Larger values are handled as follows. The least significant seven bits of each byte encode the data, and the most significant bit is used to indicate whether there are...
requirements.mapping_reason: Both read and write paths for Variable Byte Integer are implemented correctly in the shared mqttbytes-core primitives, and v5 re-exports them directly. The encoding uses 7 data bits per byte with bit 7 as the continuation flag, limits to 4 bytes max, and caps at 268435455. v5 also uses the same VBI decoder for property lengths via the length() wrapper.

evidence.reason: length() reads VBI using continuation bit at position 7, accumulates 7 bits per byte with shift increments of 7, rejects more than 4 bytes (shift > 21), and returns (byte_count, decoded_value). write_remaining_length() encodes using modulo 128 for 7 data bits, sets bit 7 as continuation flag, and rejects values > 268435455. len_len() correctly maps value ranges to 1-4 byte encodings.
v5 module re-exports core VBI primitives directly with no wrapping or modification that could alter the encoding scheme.

test_coverage.notes: Core primitive tests cover VBI threshold boundaries (0, 127, 128, 16384, 2097152) and round-trip encode/decode for representative values including 0, 127, 128, 321, 16384, and 268435455.

requirements.summary: Both strings MUST comply with the requirements for UTF-8 Encoded Strings . If a receiver (Client or Server) receives a string pair which does not meet these requirements it is a Malformed Packet. Refer to section 4.13 for information about handling errors.
requirements.mapping_reason: UTF-8 String Pairs appear as UserProperty values in MQTT v5 properties. Every staged UserProperty read path reads the key and value with two consecutive read_mqtt_string calls, and every staged UserProperty write path writes the key and value with two consecutive write_mqtt_string calls. Those shared helpers delegate to validate_mqtt_string, enforcing well-formed UTF-8, U+0000 rejection, and surrogate rejection (U+D800-U+DFFF). A malformed string in either half of the pair causes the property read/write to fail with MalformedPacket or TopicNotUtf8.

evidence.reason: UserProperty read: two consecutive read_mqtt_string() calls (key then value), each validating UTF-8. UserProperty write: two consecutive write_mqtt_string() calls, each validating UTF-8 before serializing.
CONNECT and Will UserProperty pairs use two read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string calls, each enforcing UTF-8 validation.
AUTH UserProperty pairs use the same key-then-value read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string pattern.
CONNACK UserProperty pairs use read_mqtt_string for key and value on decode and write_mqtt_string for key and value on encode.
DISCONNECT UserProperty pairs use the same key-then-value read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string pattern.
PUBACK UserProperty pairs use the same key-then-value read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string pattern.
PUBREC UserProperty pairs use the same key-then-value read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string pattern.
PUBREL UserProperty pairs use the same key-then-value read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string pattern.
PUBCOMP UserProperty pairs use the same key-then-value read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string pattern.

UNSUBSCRIBE UserProperty pairs use the same key-then-value read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string pattern.
validate_mqtt_string rejects ill-formed UTF-8 (including surrogates) and U+0000. read_mqtt_string delegates to it. v5 write_mqtt_string validates before writing.

test_coverage.notes: UserProperty tests cover valid string-pair parsing, malformed UTF-8 in either key or value, U+0000 rejection on read, and U+0000 rejection on write. Shared primitive tests cover surrogate and other malformed UTF-8 encodings.

requirements.summary: The remaining bits of byte 1 in the Fixed Header contain flags specific to each MQTT Control Packet type as shown below. Where a flag bit is marked as “Reserved”, it is reserved for future use and MUST be set to the value listed .
requirements.mapping_reason: The v5 Packet::read path now validates reserved flag bits in the fixed header via validate_fixed_header_flags(), ported from v4 with the addition of the Auth packet type. The function checks: flags==0 for most types, flags==0b0010 for SUBSCRIBE/UNSUBSCRIBE/PUBREL, and any flags for PUBLISH. IncorrectPacketFormat is returned internally on violation. InboundDisconnect::classify maps IncorrectPacketFormat to DisconnectReasonCode::MalformedPacket because MQTT v5 section 2.1.3 classifies invalid fixed-header flags as a Malformed Packet. Disconnect::read also preserves standalone public-parser validation for packet type and flags. The write side was already compliant (all hardcoded first bytes use correct flag values).

evidence.reason: validate_fixed_header_flags() checks byte1 & 0x0F against expected values per packet type (0 for most, 0b0010 for SUBSCRIBE/UNSUBSCRIBE/PUBREL, any for PUBLISH). Packet::read calls it after packet_type() and before dispatching to individual packet readers. FixedHeader::flags() provides a convenience accessor.
Disconnect::read validates packet type and reserved fixed-header flags itself, preserving correctness for direct callers of this public parser. Invalid DISCONNECT flags return IncorrectPacketFormat.

IncorrectPacketFormat is mapped to DisconnectReasonCode::MalformedPacket. MQTT v5 section 2.1.3 states that invalid fixed-header flags are a Malformed Packet; section 4.13 then uses reason code 0x81 for Malformed Packet.

test_coverage.notes: Each non-PUBLISH packet type has a dedicated test for incorrect reserved fixed-header flags. PUBLISH is verified to accept any flag bits at the fixed-header level (QoS 3 is still rejected later by Publish::read). The const fn is smoke-tested for both valid and invalid inputs. Integration tests in framed.rs verify that IncorrectPacketFormat triggers a DISCONNECT with MalformedPacket reason code.

requirements.summary: A PUBLISH packet MUST NOT contain a Packet Identifier if its QoS value is set to 0 .
requirements.mapping_reason: Encode path: Publish::write() skips pkid serialization entirely when qos == AtMostOnce, so no pkid bytes appear on the wire for QoS 0. Decode path: Publish::read() sets pkid=0 without consuming bytes from the stream for QoS 0. State layer: outgoing_publish_with_notice() does not assign or track a pkid for QoS 0 publishes. Length calculation: Publish::len() excludes the 2-byte pkid field for QoS 0. A QoS 0 Publish struct with a non-zero pkid has its pkid silently ignored at encode time rather than explicitly rejected, but the wire format is still correct.

evidence.reason: Encode: write() skips pkid serialization for QoS::AtMostOnce. Decode: read() sets pkid=0 without reading from stream for QoS::AtMostOnce. Length: len() excludes pkid bytes for QoS 0.
State layer does not assign or track a pkid for QoS 0 publishes (guarded by `if publish.qos != QoS::AtMostOnce`).

test_coverage.notes: length_calculation verifies size/write consistency for QoS 0. qos0_publish_encoding_omits_packet_identifier_even_if_struct_has_pkid explicitly constructs a QoS 0 Publish with a non-zero struct pkid and verifies the encoded wire bytes omit the packet identifier.

requirements.summary: Each time a Client sends a new SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE,or PUBLISH (where QoS > 0) MQTT Control Packet it MUST assign it a non-zero Packet Identifier that is currently unused .
requirements.mapping_reason: Client-side non-zero pkid assignment is enforced in both the codec and state layers for SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE, and PUBLISH QoS > 0. Publish, Subscribe, and Unsubscribe read/write paths reject pkid==0. next_control_pkid assigns non-zero currently-unused pkids for subscribe/unsubscribe requests. next_publish_pkid and next_pkid assign currently-unused publish pkids, with next_pkid returning None instead of recycling an in-use pkid when the publish pkid range is exhausted. outgoing_publish_with_notice validates pkid bounds, preserves outgoing_pub collision replay handling, and rejects pkids already used by outgoing_rel, tracked_subscribe, or tracked_unsubscribe.

evidence.reason: Publish encode and decode reject pkid==0 for QoS > 0 with Error::PacketIdZero.
Subscribe encode and decode reject pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.
Unsubscribe encode and decode reject pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.

Packet id assignment checks packet_identifier_in_use across outgoing_pub, outgoing_rel, tracked_subscribe, and tracked_unsubscribe. next_pkid returns None when no currently-unused publish pkid is available.

Subscribe and unsubscribe requests receive pkids from next_control_pkid. Outgoing publish validates pkid bounds and rejects cross-type conflicts with pubrel, subscribe, and unsubscribe tracking before storing a new outgoing publish.

test_coverage.notes: State tests cover pkid increment, exhausted publish-id allocation, max-inflight behavior, and cross-type conflicts with subscribe, unsubscribe, and pubrel tracking. Packet codec tests cover zero-pkid rejection for PUBLISH QoS > 0, SUBSCRIBE, and UNSUBSCRIBE.

requirements.summary: Each time a Server sends a new PUBLISH (with QoS > 0) MQTT Control Packet it MUST assign it a non zero Packet Identifier that is currently unused .
requirements.mapping_reason: This is a server-only obligation: the server must assign unused packet identifiers for outgoing PUBLISH QoS>0. A client does not need to validate that server-assigned packet IDs are unique. The client does validate pkid!=0 on inbound PUBLISH QoS>0 (related to MQTT-2.2.1-3), and tracks incoming packet IDs for its own acknowledgment flow, but that is not this requirement.

evidence.reason: Validates pkid!=0 for QoS>0 on inbound PUBLISH (MQTT-2.2.1-3, not this requirement). Does not validate uniqueness of server-assigned IDs.
Tracks incoming packet IDs in FixedBitSets for acknowledgment flow, not for validating server uniqueness compliance.

test_coverage.notes: Server-only requirement; no client-side test needed for this specific obligation.

requirements.follow_up: No follow-up needed. The client correctly validates pkid!=0 (MQTT-2.2.1-3) and tracks IDs for ack flow, which is all a client must do.

requirements.summary: A PUBACK, PUBREC , PUBREL, or PUBCOMP packet MUST contain the same Packet Identifier as the PUBLISH packet that was originally sent . A SUBACK and UNSUBACK MUST contain the Packet Identifier that was used in the corresponding SUBSCRIBE and UNSUBSCRIBE packet respectively .
requirements.mapping_reason: Client-generated acks correctly echo the pkid from the received packet: automatic PUBACK/PUBREC use publish.pkid, automatic PUBREL uses pubrec.pkid, and automatic PUBCOMP uses pubrel.pkid. Manual/outgoing PUBACK and PUBREC are validated against tracked incoming QoS1/QoS2 publish packet identifiers; outgoing PUBREL is validated against packet identifiers for QoS2 publishes that reached PUBREC or are being replayed after reconnect. Explicit outbound PUBLISH packet identifiers are also rejected while reserved for PUBREL replay. Client validates incoming PUBACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP pkids against tracked packet-id state (returns StateError::Unsolicited on mismatch). Incoming SUBACK and UNSUBACK pkids are validated against tracked_subscribe/tracked_unsubscribe. All ack decode paths (PubAck::read, PubRec::read, PubRel::read, PubComp::read, SubAck::read, UnsubAck::read) reject pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero. All v5 PUBACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP encode paths also reject pkid==0.

evidence.reason: Client-generated acks correctly echo pkid: PubAck::new(publish.pkid, None) for QoS1, PubRec::new(pkid, None) for QoS2, PubRel::new(pubrec.pkid, None) for PUBREL, PubComp::new(pubrel.pkid, None) for PUBCOMP.

Manual/outgoing PUBACK and PUBREC are rejected with StateError::Unsolicited unless their pkid matches a tracked incoming QoS1/QoS2 PUBLISH packet identifier. Explicit outbound PUBLISH pkids are rejected while reserved for PUBREL replay. Outgoing PUBREL is rejected unless the pkid is already in outgoing_rel or the reconnect replay set.
Incoming PUBACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP are validated by packet identifier: return StateError::Unsolicited if pkid doesn't match tracked outgoing/incoming packet-id state.
Incoming SUBACK/UNSUBACK pkids are validated against tracked_subscribe/tracked_unsubscribe. Returns StateError::Unsolicited if pkid doesn't match any tracked request. Notice is resolved on match.
SubAck::read rejects pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.
UnsubAck::read rejects pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.
PubAck::read and PubAck::write reject pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.
PubRec::read and PubRec::write reject pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.
PubRel::read and PubRel::write reject pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.
PubComp::read and PubComp::write reject pkid==0 with Error::PacketIdZero.

requirements.summary: The Property Length is encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. The Property Length does not include the bytes used to encode itself, but includes the length of the Properties. If there are no properties, this MUST be indicated by including a Property Length of zero .
requirements.mapping_reason: Property Length is encoded as a Variable Byte Integer using write_remaining_length (the same VBI encoder used for remaining length). Packet property writers compute their property payload length and then call write_remaining_length(buffer, len). When a property section is present but contains no properties, writers encode Property Length 0. Some MQTT v5 packet forms, such as successful PUBACK without properties, legally omit the reason code and property length entirely; those compact forms are separate from explicit zero-length property sections. Property readers call length() before parsing property fields and return Ok(None) when properties_len == 0 for optional property structs.

evidence.reason: write_remaining_length encodes a Variable Byte Integer; length() decodes one. Both are used for property length encoding/decoding.

Publish::write calls write_remaining_length(buffer, 0) when the PUBLISH property section is absent, and PublishProperties::write calls write_remaining_length(buffer, len) when present. PublishProperties::read uses length() to decode the VBI property length and returns Ok(None) when properties_len==0.

CONNECT, Will, and their property structs encode property lengths with write_remaining_length(), including zero for absent/empty property sections where the packet format requires a property length field, and decode property lengths with length().
AUTH properties compute the property payload length, emit it with write_remaining_length(), and decode it with length().
CONNACK properties compute the property payload length, emit it with write_remaining_length(), and decode the property section length with length() before parsing properties.
DISCONNECT properties compute the property payload length, emit it with write_remaining_length(), and decode it with length().

PUBACK properties compute the property payload length, emit it with write_remaining_length(), and decode it with length(). PUBACK also supports the MQTT v5 compact success form that omits reason code and property length when there are no properties.

PUBREL properties compute the property payload length, emit it with write_remaining_length(), and decode it with length().

SUBSCRIBE properties compute the property payload length, emit it with write_remaining_length(), and decode it with length().
UNSUBSCRIBE properties compute the property payload length, emit it with write_remaining_length(), and decode it with length().
The check function uses core_primitives::parse_fixed_header which calls length() for VBI decoding of remaining length, the same primitive used for property lengths.

test_coverage.notes: Core VBI encoding/decoding is tested directly. Module-qualified length_calculation tests avoid ambiguity between repeated test names and verify packet/property size accounting for representative property-bearing packets. The explicit zero-property-length tests verify that every property-bearing packet form emits and decodes a zero Property Length where the packet format requires one, while preserving compact no-property encodings where MQTT v5 allows omission.

requirements.summary: After a Network Connection is established by a Client to a Server, the first packet sent from the Client to the Server MUST be a CONNECT packet .
requirements.mapping_reason: The v5 eventloop architecturally guarantees CONNECT is the first packet sent on every new network connection. When self.network is None, poll() calls connect() which first establishes the TCP/TLS/WS transport via network_connect() and then immediately sends CONNECT via mqtt_connect() before returning. No other packet can be sent before CONNECT because: (1) the network object does not exist until connect() returns, (2) the Request enum has no Connect variant so users cannot inject a CONNECT through request channels, and (3) the select() method (which processes user requests) only runs after self.network = Some(network). The eventloop also validates that the first server response is CONNACK or AUTH, returning ConnectionError::NotConnAck otherwise.

evidence.reason: poll() calls connect() when network is None. connect() calls network_connect() then mqtt_connect(), which sends CONNECT as the very first MQTT packet on the new transport. No other packet path can execute before self.network is set.

mqtt_connect_inner() constructs a Connect packet and writes it to the network, then awaits a ConnAck or AUTH response. ConnectionError::NotConnAck is returned if the first response is not ConnAck or AUTH.
The Request enum has no Connect variant, so users cannot submit a CONNECT packet through the request channel. CONNECT is only sent internally by mqtt_connect_inner().

test_coverage.notes: Wire-level test verifies the first packet sent by mqtt_connect is CONNECT. Eventloop-level test queues a request before connection establishment and verifies CONNECT is sent before the queued request on the same socket.

requirements.summary: A Client can only send the CONNECT packet once over a Network Connection. The Server MUST process a second CONNECT packet sent from a Client as a Protocol Error and close the Network Connection . Refer to section 4.13 for information about handling errors.
requirements.mapping_reason: The client cannot send a second CONNECT on the same network connection. The Request enum has no Connect variant, so users cannot submit a CONNECT through request channels. The CONNECT packet is only sent internally by mqtt_connect_inner(), which is called exactly once per connection attempt inside connect(), which itself only runs when self.network is None. After a successful connection, self.network = Some(network) and subsequent poll() calls go to select(), which never calls mqtt_connect() again. When the connection is lost, self.clean() sets self.network = None, and the next poll() call re-establishes a new network connection (new TCP socket) before sending CONNECT again. The server-side obligation (MUST close on second CONNECT) is outside client scope.

evidence.reason: The Request enum has no Connect variant (15 variants: Publish, PubAck, PubRec, PubComp, PubRel, PingReq, PingResp, Subscribe, SubAck, Unsubscribe, UnsubAck, Auth, Disconnect, DisconnectNow, DisconnectWithTimeout), making it impossible for users to submit a CONNECT packet through the request channel.

mqtt_connect_inner() is the function that writes the CONNECT packet, and it is reached only through mqtt_connect() during connect(). connect() only runs from poll() when self.network is None. After connect() succeeds, self.network is set and mqtt_connect_inner() is never called again on the same connection.
When the connection is lost, clean() sets self.network = None, causing the next poll() to establish a completely new network connection before sending CONNECT again.

test_coverage.notes: Variant-enumeration test documents the current Request surface while the Request enum definition provides the compile-time evidence that no Connect variant exists. Eventloop-level wire test verifies that, after CONNECT succeeds, a later queued request on the same socket is sent as that request rather than as a second CONNECT.

requirements.summary: A Server which support multiple protocols uses the Protocol Name to determine whether the data is MQTT. The protocol name MUST be the UTF-8 String "MQTT". If the Server does not want to accept the CONNECT, and wishes to reveal that it is an MQTT Server it MAY send a CONNACK pa...
requirements.mapping_reason: This requirement includes the client-relevant wire-field rule that the CONNECT protocol name MUST be the UTF-8 string "MQTT". The v5 encoder always writes the correct protocol name in Connect::write() via write_mqtt_string(buffer, "MQTT"), so callers cannot construct an outbound CONNECT with any other protocol name. The requirement also describes server behavior for an incorrect protocol name; that server-side reaction is outside rumqttc client scope. The decode path (Connect::read) defensively validates the protocol name and returns Error::InvalidProtocol when it is not "MQTT".

evidence.reason: Encode path always writes 'MQTT' as the protocol name via write_mqtt_string(buffer, "MQTT"), guaranteeing the correct protocol name on the wire.
Decode path validates protocol_name != 'MQTT' and returns Error::InvalidProtocol, but this is only relevant if a client were to read a CONNECT packet, which it never does in normal operation.

test_coverage.notes: Encode test serializes a v5 CONNECT and verifies the protocol-name field bytes are the MQTT UTF-8 string.

requirements.summary: A Server which supports multiple versions of the MQTT protocol uses the Protocol Version to determine which version of MQTT the Client is using. If the Protocol Version is not 5 and the Server does not want to accept the CONNECT packet, the Server MAY send a CONNACK packet wit...
requirements.mapping_reason: Client encode path: Connect::write() hardcodes buffer.put_u8(0x05) with no conditional branching, so the v5 client always sends protocol version 5. The Connect struct has no protocol field that callers can modify. Decode path: Connect::read() checks protocol_level != 5 and returns Error::InvalidProtocolLevel. The client also handles CONNACK UnsupportedProtocolVersion (reason code 0x84) via the eventloop's connection error path. The obligation is MAY (server may reject), so the client-side requirement is simply to send the correct version, which it does.

evidence.reason: Connect::write() hardcodes 0x05 as the protocol version byte with no conditional. Connect::read() rejects protocol_level != 5 with Error::InvalidProtocolLevel.
mqtt_connect_inner() constructs Connect directly without any protocol version field; the write method always emits 0x05.
ConnAck decode maps reason code 132 (0x84) to UnsupportedProtocolVersion. The eventloop treats non-Success ConnAck as a connection error.

test_coverage.notes: Dedicated tests cover Connect::read() rejecting invalid protocol level 4 and Connect::write() emitting 0x05.

requirements.follow_up: No follow-up needed. Dedicated tests added for protocol version encoding and decode rejection.

requirements.summary: The Server MUST validate that the reserved flag in the CONNECT packet is set to 0 . If the reserved flag is not 0 it is a Malformed Packet. Refer to section 4.13 for information about handling errors.
requirements.mapping_reason: This requirement is server-side because it says the Server must validate malformed CONNECT flags and handle the Malformed Packet. The client crate cannot implement the server-side error-handling obligation. Client-side packet behavior is correct: Connect::write() starts connect_flags at 0 and only ORs in defined bits (clean_start=0x02, will flag=0x04, will QoS bits 3-4, will retain=0x20, username=0x80, password=0x40), so bit 0 (reserved) is always 0. requirements.summary: The Client and Server MUST store the Session State after the Network Connection is closed if the Session Expiry Interval is greater than 0 .

On reconnect, EventLoop::reconcile_connack_session() preserves local pending/session state when clean_start=false and the broker reports session_present=true; it resets local state when the broker reports session_present=false or when clean_start=true. The EventLoop also tracks the ClientID associated with retained local state and clears that state before reconnecting with a different ClientID, preventing session state from one MQTT identity from being reused under another.

reconcile_connack_session() preserves pending local state when clean_start=false and session_present=true, and resets state when the broker reports no resumed session. reset_session_state_if_client_id_changed() prevents retained local state from crossing ClientID boundaries. On connection loss, EventLoop::clean() moves unacknowledged outgoing state into the pending queue for replay on a resumed session.
MqttState holds client-side session state, including outgoing QoS state that can be cleaned into pending requests for reconnect/replay when the session is resumed.
